title: Trauma and the Immune System: How Unhealed Trauma Affects Physical Health
excerpt: Unhealed trauma disrupts the immune system, leading to chronic illness and inflammation. Understand the science linking trauma to physical health outcomes.
body:
Introduction: Beyond the Mind—Trauma’s Physical Footprint

Trauma isn’t just a psychological event stored in the past—its imprint lives in your body, influencing your health long after the triggering event. Many who have tried therapy still find themselves stuck, unable to fully recover or understand why their body reacts in unpredictable ways. The missing piece often lies in how trauma affects the immune system, the body's frontline defense against illness and injury.

Groundbreaking research in neuroscience and somatic healing reveals that unhealed trauma keeps the nervous system locked in a state of dysregulation, which in turn disrupts immune function. This article will lay out how trauma and the immune system are interconnected and why healing trauma matters not only for mental well-being but for physical health, too.

What Happens to the Immune System After Trauma?

When you experience trauma—whether a one-time event or chronic stress—the body activates its survival mechanisms. Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory explains how the autonomic nervous system shifts into states of hyperarousal (fight or flight) or hypoarousal (freeze or shutdown) to protect you. While these responses are adaptive in the moment, prolonged nervous system dysregulation creates a chronic stress environment.

Chronic stress triggers the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis, leading to sustained cortisol release. Paradoxically, while cortisol initially suppresses immune activity to prevent excessive inflammation, over time, the immune system becomes less sensitive to cortisol’s regulatory signals, leading to inflammation and immune dysfunction (Bessel van der Kolk, 2014).

In other words, trauma primes the immune system for an overactive inflammatory response, which can contribute to autoimmune disorders, allergies, and increased susceptibility to infections.

Key Immune Changes Linked to Trauma

  • Increased pro-inflammatory cytokines: Trauma survivors often show elevated levels of cytokines like IL-6 and TNF-alpha, which drive systemic inflammation (Gabor Maté, 2018).
  • Reduced natural killer (NK) cell activity: NK cells are critical for viral defense; their suppression has been linked to PTSD and chronic trauma (Dan Siegel, 2012).
  • Altered immune cell distribution: Trauma affects white blood cell counts and function, impairing the body's ability to respond appropriately to threats (Peter Levine, 2010).

The Science Behind Trauma’s Impact on Immunity

Francine Shapiro’s work with EMDR and Pat Ogden’s somatic approaches emphasize that trauma’s effects are deeply embodied, not just cognitive. But what does the scientific literature say about immune changes in trauma survivors?

A key study published in Brain, Behavior, and Immunity found that individuals with PTSD exhibited consistently higher inflammatory markers compared to controls, even years after trauma exposure (O'Donovan et al., 2015). This chronic inflammation is associated with increased risk of cardiovascular disease, diabetes, and neurodegeneration.

Moreover, Bessel van der Kolk (2014) highlights how trauma rewires the brain’s limbic system, including the amygdala and hippocampus, which modulate stress and immune responses. Dysregulation in these brain areas perpetuates heightened immune activation and hinders healing.

How Nervous System Dysregulation Drives Immune Dysfunction

Deb Dana, a leading clinician applying Polyvagal Theory, explains that trauma keeps the autonomic nervous system trapped in maladaptive states that interfere with immune regulation. When the vagus nerve’s calming influence (parasympathetic tone) is low, pro-inflammatory pathways dominate.

For example, a person stuck in fight/flight may have elevated heart rate and cortisol levels, which disrupts the balance of immune signaling. Conversely, a person in shutdown or dissociation can have suppressed immune vigilance, increasing vulnerability to infection.

Dan Siegel’s concept of interpersonal neurobiology reinforces that the nervous system and immune system are interconnected through bi-directional signaling pathways. Healing trauma means restoring nervous system regulation, which in turn normalizes immune function.

“Trauma is not just an event of the past; it lives inside your body and can silently undermine your immune system, making it harder to heal physically and emotionally.” — The Oracle Lover

Common Physical Health Conditions Linked to Unhealed Trauma

Understanding trauma’s role in physical illness reframes many chronic conditions not as isolated medical issues but as manifestations of underlying nervous system and immune dysregulation.

  • Autoimmune diseases: Disorders like lupus, rheumatoid arthritis, and multiple sclerosis have been linked to chronic trauma and stress-induced inflammation (Gabor Maté, 2018).
  • Chronic pain and fibromyalgia: Heightened nervous system sensitivity and inflammatory responses exacerbate pain syndromes common in trauma survivors (Peter Levine, 2010).
  • Gastrointestinal disorders: Conditions such as irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) are often triggered or worsened by trauma via the brain-gut-immune axis (Bessel van der Kolk, 2014).
  • Cardiovascular disease: Chronic inflammation and stress hormones contribute to hypertension and heart disease risk (O'Donovan et al., 2015).

Somatic Healing Strategies to Restore Immune Balance

Traditional talk therapy alone may not resolve trauma’s impact on the immune system because the body’s survival responses are nonverbal and stored in neural and cellular memory. Somatic approaches focus on restoring nervous system regulation, which has downstream benefits for immune health.

Effective Somatic Practices Include:

  • Polyvagal-informed interventions: Practices that enhance vagal tone like paced breathing, gentle movement, and social engagement help reduce inflammation (Deb Dana, 2018).
  • Body awareness and tracking sensations: Peter Levine’s Somatic Experiencing teaches how to notice bodily sensations and discharge trapped survival energy safely (Levine, 2010).
  • Mindful movement: Yoga, tai chi, and qigong combine awareness with movement to calm the nervous system and modulate immune responses.
  • Trauma-informed mindfulness: Mindfulness practices adapted for trauma help regulate the stress response without overwhelming the nervous system (Richard Schwartz, 2013).

How to Support Your Immune System While Healing Trauma

In addition to somatic healing, supporting immune health requires lifestyle habits that reduce chronic stress and inflammation:

  • Sleep hygiene: Quality sleep is critical for immune resilience and nervous system regulation.
  • Nutrition: Anti-inflammatory diets rich in omega-3s, antioxidants, and whole foods support immune balance.
  • Safe social connection: Positive relationships activate the social engagement system, increasing vagal tone (Deb Dana, 2018).
  • Regular physical activity: Movement reduces stress hormones and improves immune function.
  • Professional support: Somatic therapists, trauma-informed practitioners, and integrative medicine providers can guide safe healing.

Conclusion: Healing Trauma is Healing Your Body

Science is unequivocal: trauma lives in your body and shapes your immune system. Unhealed trauma leads to chronic inflammation, immune dysfunction, and increased risk of physical illness. Understanding this connection empowers you to take a holistic approach to healing—one that integrates nervous system regulation, somatic awareness, and lifestyle support.

By honoring trauma’s impact on your body and engaging in trauma-informed somatic healing, you are not just healing emotional wounds—you are restoring your immune system’s capacity to protect and repair. This is the foundation for lasting health and resilience.

References:

  • Bessel van der Kolk, The Body Keeps the Score, 2014.
  • Peter Levine, Waking the Tiger, 2010.
  • Stephen Porges, The Polyvagal Theory, 2011.
  • Deb Dana, The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy, 2018.
  • Gabor Maté, When the Body Says No, 2018.
  • Dan Siegel, Mindsight, 2012.
  • Pat Ogden, Trauma and the Body, 2015.
  • Francine Shapiro, EMDR Research, 2014.
  • O'Donovan et al., "Elevated inflammation in PTSD," Brain, Behavior, and Immunity, 2015.
  • Richard Schwartz, Internal Family Systems Therapy, 2013.

faqs.q: How does trauma cause inflammation in the body?
faqs.a: Trauma triggers chronic stress responses, leading to dysregulation of the HPA axis and sustained release of stress hormones. This causes immune cells to produce elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines, resulting in systemic inflammation.

faqs.q: Can healing trauma improve immune function?
faqs.a: Yes. Somatic healing and nervous system regulation techniques help restore autonomic balance, which normalizes immune responses and reduces chronic inflammation.

faqs.q: Why do some people with trauma develop autoimmune diseases?
faqs.a: Chronic immune activation from unhealed trauma can cause the immune system to mistakenly attack healthy tissues, leading to autoimmune conditions.

faqs.q: Is talk therapy enough to heal trauma’s impact on the body?
faqs.a: Talk therapy alone often misses the nonverbal, somatic aspects of trauma stored in the body. Integrating somatic approaches is crucial for full nervous system and immune healing.

affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book explaining how trauma affects the brain and body, essential for understanding somatic healing.
